gm.name: Monti Ossenfort
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Director of Player Personnel, Tennessee Titans

career_path.mentor: Bill Belichick / Scott Pioli / Thomas Dimitroff (scouting director at the time)

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Bill Belichick (New England Patriots, 15 combined seasons across two stints). Belichick was the de facto GM during Ossenfort's entire Patriots tenure, making him the dominant influence on Ossenfort's scouting and evaluation philosophy.
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Belichick tree via Scott Pioli pathway. Pioli's call brought Ossenfort to New England in 2003. The Belichick tree emphasizes positional versatility, character, team-first culture, willingness to trade down and accumulate picks, and a heavy premium on special teams contributors even on Day 3. Secondary influence from Charley Casserly in Houston, known for systematic evaluation processes. Tertiary influence from Jon Robinson in Tennessee, who was himself a Belichick tree disciple — Robinson was NE's director of college scouting before Ossenfort replaced him in 2014.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Ossenfort has shown a stronger willingness to pick BPA at the very top of the draft than the typical Belichick-tree GM, who historically preferred trading down from premium positions. At pick 4 in 2024, Ossenfort stayed put for Marvin Harrison Jr. despite exploring trade-back options — the classic Belichick move would have been to trade down. He has also shown more willingness to draft injured players who slide (Williams with torn ACL, Will Johnson with knee concerns) than the traditionally conservative Belichick medical approach. However, his love of trading and accumulating picks in his first draft (2023) was pure Belichick DNA.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: 3 drafts of data as Cardinals GM bring lineage weight down substantially. Ossenfort has shown enough of his own tendencies — willingness to stay at top of draft, preference for sliding talent with medical flags, heavy defensive investment — to override pure lineage prediction. Will drop to 0.20 after 2026 draft.

key_influencers.name: Dave Sears
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Works closely with Ossenfort on final board construction and draft-day decisions. Co-leads post-draft press conferences. Trusted lieutenant in the scouting and evaluation process.

key_influencers.name: Mike LaFleur
key_influencers.title: Head Coach (hired February 1, 2026)
key_influencers.known_biases: Shanahan/McVay offensive tree coach. Will likely prioritize offensive line athleticism and versatility, zone-run scheme fits, and versatile receiving weapons. This is his first draft with the Cardinals — influence level is projected, not confirmed. Retained DC Nick Rallis from prior staff, suggesting defensive scheme continuity while offense changes. Nathaniel Hackett is OC but LaFleur will call plays.

key_influencers.name: Nick Rallis
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator (retained from Gannon staff)
key_influencers.known_biases: Runs a scheme that emphasizes versatility along the defensive front and zone coverage principles in the secondary. Has been with the Cardinals for three seasons under Gannon, so there is philosophical alignment with how the defense has been built.

key_influencers.name: Michael Bidwill
key_influencers.title: Owner/Chairman
key_influencers.known_biases: Involved in major decisions (GM hire, HC hire), participated in draft phone calls to drafted players. Pushed for 'ego-free' culture when hiring Ossenfort. Not known to override football decisions on draft day but stays informed.

front_office.power_dynamics: Collaborative front office where the GM has final say on personnel. Ossenfort has been the clear decision-maker across all three drafts. He hired Gannon, fired Gannon, and hired LaFleur — all with Bidwill's backing but with Ossenfort leading the process. LaFleur is a new hire and the power dynamic between GM and HC on draft decisions is TBD for 2026, but Ossenfort's institutional control is well-established. Bidwill described the structure as aligned between ownership, GM, and HC during Ossenfort's introductory presser.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Ossenfort has consistently stated he is looking for 'good players and good people' who fit culturally and on the field. He said in 2024: 'Our list of needs is good players that fit us.' He emphasizes that the game tape is 'the vast majority' of the evaluation pie, with combine testing, medical, and interviews filling in the rest.
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Leans BPA in round 1 but within the context of clear team needs. In 2023, he traded down from 3 and still picked Paris Johnson Jr. at OT (a need position but also a BPA-caliber pick). In 2024, he stayed at 4 for Marvin Harrison Jr. — the consensus #1 non-QB, who also addressed the team's biggest need at WR. In 2025, he took Walter Nolen (DT) at 16 to continue the defensive rebuild. His picks typically align with both BPA and need because he's in a full rebuild where every position is a need. However, his 2024 draft is most revealing: at pick 27 he took Darius Robinson (EDGE/DL) when CBs like Cooper DeJean were available — he chose the player with the most positional versatility and scheme fit rather than the higher-consensus CB. In rounds 2-3, he shifts toward need-filling: 3 CBs in the 2024 draft, 2 CBs in 2025, reflecting his willingness to stack a position of need.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Stated philosophy aligns reasonably well with actual behavior. Ossenfort genuinely appears to lean BPA but during a rebuild, BPA and need often converge. The biggest reveal is his willingness to reach slightly for scheme-versatile players (Robinson at 27 in 2024 was viewed by some analysts as a slight reach). He also clearly values sliding talent over pure positional need — taking Will Johnson at 47 in 2025 when the position was already being addressed.

trade_triggers: Falling elite talent that slid past their projected range (Will Johnson in 2025 — stayed put and took him rather than trade back despite offers)
Opportunity to accumulate future first-round picks (2023 trade from 3 to Houston netted a future 1st)
Situational value — Ossenfort has said decisions are 'opportunity-based' rather than pre-planned
Clear tier break on board where trading back still nets a target player
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Ossenfort's Belichick DNA shows most clearly in trade behavior. In his first draft (2023), he executed a masterclass: traded from 3 to 12 (gaining Houston's 2024 1st and additional picks), then traded back up from 12 to 6 to get his guy (Paris Johnson Jr.). Net result: got his top target AND gained a future first-round pick and multiple Day 2 picks. In 2024, with 11 picks including two 1sts, he used the capital rather than hoarding. In 2025, he eschewed trade-back offers at picks 47 and 78 because he had specific players he wanted. He values future picks but stated 'we need players' and won't trade back purely to hoard. Prefers current-year players when the board falls favorably. From his Patriots background: New England made 46 draft-day trades from 2010-21, nearly all during Ossenfort's tenure — 29 trade-downs, 16 trade-ups.

position_avoidance: QB — Has not drafted a QB in rounds 1-3 in three drafts. Clayton Tune was a Day 3 developmental pick in 2023 (5th round). Now releasing Kyler Murray and heading into 2026 draft at pick 3 with massive QB need — this may change dramatically.
S — Has not used premium draft capital on safety. Dadrion Taylor-Demerson was a 4th-rounder in 2024, Kitan Crawford a 7th-rounder in 2025. Relies on Budda Baker as cornerstone.
TE — Only one TE drafted (Tip Reiman, 3rd round 2024) and he was a blocking-first selection.
position_premium: CB — Has drafted 7 cornerbacks across 3 drafts (Williams, Clark, Melton, Elijah Jones, Jaden Davis, Will Johnson, Denzel Burke). Massive investment reflecting the Gannon DB-coach influence. Even without Gannon, Rallis retained.

DL/EDGE — Drafted Darius Robinson (1.27, 2024), Xavier Thomas (5.138, 2024), Walter Nolen (1.16, 2025), Jordan Burch (3.78, 2025). Two consecutive first-round defensive line picks. Also BJ Ojulari (2.41, 2023).
OT — Paris Johnson Jr. was the very first pick of the Ossenfort era at 6th overall. Christian Jones and Isaiah Adams also added as depth.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Versatile, high-motor defensive linemen who can align at multiple positions along the front — both inside and on the edge. Ossenfort values scheme versatility, length, and the ability to affect both the pass and run game. Not seeking pure speed rushers; prefers physical, power-based players who win with effort and functional strength. Think big, long players who can kick inside on passing downs — Darius Robinson is the prototype, not a pure finesse bend rusher.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches preferred based on Robinson (34.5 inches) and Ojulari (34 inches). Burch at 6-4.25 with massive frame. Length is clearly valued.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Values explosiveness (Robinson: 35-inch vert, 9-foot-3 broad). Motor and effort rank higher than pure timed speed. Short-area quickness to counter inside matters.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary factor. Robinson ran 4.95 at 285 lbs — speed is secondary to power and versatility. Ojulari ran 4.59 at 248 — faster, but valued for motor and production over speed alone.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Positional versatility and power at the point of attack. Robinson played DT before switching to edge. Burch can play multiple alignment techniques (5, 6, 9-tech). Ossenfort explicitly stated he values Burch's 'unique skillset for a guy who is his size and length and to be able to play on his feet and play on the edge.'
most_valued_traits: Alignment versatility — can play inside and outside
Power and functional strength at POA
High motor and relentless effort
Length and ability to control blockers with arm extension
Run defense ability — Ossenfort wants three-down players
least_important_traits: Pure speed-to-power conversion off the edge — has not drafted a classic speed rusher
Polished pass-rush move repertoire — Robinson was raw in moves but had power
deal_breakers: One-dimensional speed rushers who cannot defend the run
Undersized pass-rush specialists without positional versatility
Low-motor players who take plays off
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite positional versatility. Robinson at pick 27 was considered a mild reach by some analysts who had him as a late-1st/early-2nd prospect. The DT-to-edge versatility pushed him up Ossenfort's board.

college_production.production_floor: Ascending production trajectory preferred. Robinson had 8.5 sacks and 14 TFL in his final season. Ojulari had 16.5 career sacks in 3 years at LSU. Burch had 11.5 sacks in two seasons at Oregon. Xavier Thomas had 22 career sacks at Clemson.
college_production.production_trajectory: Strongly prefers ascending production. Robinson went from 3.5 sacks to 8.5 sacks in his final two years. Burch improved each year at Oregon.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High. Ossenfort accounts for scheme, usage, and position changes. Robinson's interior-to-edge transition was credited as development, not instability.

examples: 2023 2.41 — BJ Ojulari, LSU: 6-2, 248, 4.59 40, 34-inch arms. High-motor pass rusher with deep collection of moves, SEC production leader.
2024 1.27 — Darius Robinson, Missouri: 6-5, 285, 4.95 40, 34.5-inch arms, 35-inch vert. Power rusher with DT-to-edge versatility, First-Team All-SEC.
2024 5.138 — Xavier Thomas, Clemson: 6-2, 260. Six-year veteran with 22 career sacks, high motor.
2025 3.78 — Jordan Burch, Oregon: 6-4.25, 279. Size of a DL with edge rusher production (11.5 sacks in 2 years). Versatile alignment player.

OT.archetype_summary: Elite athletic tackles with positional versatility and scheme flexibility. Ossenfort's first-ever pick was Paris Johnson Jr. — a former guard who moved to tackle, with prototype size, elite athleticism, and the ability to play either side. Values length, movement skills, and the ability to operate in a variety of blocking schemes. Not seeking pure maulers; wants athletes who can pass protect and move in space.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches. Paris Johnson Jr. measured 36 1/8-inch arms — elite length clearly valued.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Broad jump as explosiveness proxy (Johnson: 110 inches). Bench press for functional strength (Johnson: 29 reps). Foot quickness in drills valued over 40 time.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary metric. Johnson did not run the 40 at the combine. Movement quality in OL drills matters more than timed speed.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Athletic pass protection with strong leverage and sustain in the run game. Johnson excelled in run blocking and uses leverage well despite his height.
most_valued_traits: Positional versatility — guard/tackle flexibility
Elite arm length and wingspan
Athletic movement ability in space
Leadership and character (Johnson's family legacy, journalism degree)
Ability to sustain blocks and finish
least_important_traits: Pure power/mauling ability — not seeking a road-grading run blocker only
40-yard dash time
deal_breakers: Limited movement ability — stiff tackles who cannot redirect
Short arms for the position
Character concerns — Ossenfort emphasized Johnson's leadership traits
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite combination of size, length, and athleticism. Johnson was the consensus top OT in a loaded 2023 class.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred. Johnson was a 3-year contributor at Ohio State (guard in 2021, LT in 2022). Christian Jones (5th round, 2024) had 5 seasons at Texas with 3,219 career snaps.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values multi-year experience and growth. Johnson's guard-to-tackle transition showed development arc.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High. Considers offensive system and competition level. Johnson's Ohio State pedigree and PFF metrics heavily weighted.

examples: 2023 1.6 — Paris Johnson Jr., Ohio State: 6-6 3/8, 313, 36 1/8-inch arms, 29 bench reps, 110-inch broad jump. Elite athletic tackle with guard experience. Allowed only 14 pressures in final college season.
2024 3.71 — Isaiah Adams, Illinois: OT/G convert from Canada. 81.1 run-blocking PFF grade. Athletic with tackle experience but projects inside.
2024 5.162 — Christian Jones, Texas: 5-year starter with 3,219 career snaps. Depth/developmental piece.

IOL.archetype_summary: Consistent, balanced interior linemen who can contribute in both run and pass protection. Not seeking flashy athletes; values reliability, balance, and positional versatility. The Cardinals have invested Day 3-4 picks on interior linemen who are polished and ready to contribute as depth or eventual starters.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear threshold from limited sample
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Balance and consistency in blocking grades matter more than combine numbers. Hayden Conner (2025 6th round) was 6-6, 314 — size clearly valued.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A — not a meaningful metric for IOL in Ossenfort's evaluations.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Balance and consistency across run and pass blocking. Gaines graded 76.7 in run blocking and 77.4 in pass blocking — one of only three guards in his class to grade above 76 in both.
most_valued_traits: Consistency in both pass and run blocking
Positional versatility — guard/center/tackle flexibility
Experienced college starters with high snap counts
Physical toughness and tenacity
least_important_traits: Elite athleticism or movement ability — IOL picks have been steady performers, not athletic freaks
Flashy traits in any single area
deal_breakers: One-dimensional blockers who can only run block or pass protect but not both
Lack of experience or starting reps in college
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: No evidence of reaching for IOL — all have been Day 3+ picks. Ossenfort fills IOL as depth, not premium investment.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter. Gaines was a reliable contributor. Adams had 4+ years at Illinois. Conner started 43 games at Texas.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers experienced, proven starters over raw athletic projects at this position.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values Senior Bowl and pre-draft process performance. Adams impressed coaches at the Senior Bowl.

examples: 2023 4.105 — Jon Gaines II, UCLA: Guard with balanced run (76.7 PFF) and pass blocking (77.4 PFF) grades. Reliable and consistent.
2024 3.71 — Isaiah Adams, Illinois: OT who projects as guard in NFL. 81.1 run-blocking grade. Impressed at Senior Bowl.
2025 6.211 — Hayden Conner, Texas: 6-6, 314. Versatile IOL who started 43 games. Experience and size valued.

DT.archetype_summary: Disruptive, penetrating interior defensive linemen with the athleticism to affect the quarterback from inside. Ossenfort took Walter Nolen 16th overall in 2025 — a powerful, athletic DT who earned the second-highest PFF run-defense grade in FBS (91.6) with 6.5 sacks and 14 TFL. This signals a premium on interior disruption and three-down impact. Combined with Darius Robinson's DT background, Ossenfort clearly values interior linemen who can rush the passer AND defend the run.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear hard minimum from limited sample, but length valued across defensive front
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosiveness off the snap. Nolen was graded as an elite athlete for his size. Short-area quickness to disrupt blocking schemes.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not primary. Interior quickness and burst matter more than 40 time.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Interior disruption in both run and pass situations. Nolen shed blocks and made 32 tackles within two yards of the line of scrimmage. He's a three-down player, not a specialist.
most_valued_traits: Elite run defense and ability to hold the point of attack
Interior pass-rush ability — sack production from DT alignment
Ability to shed blocks and make plays at/behind the LOS
Motor and effort play-to-play
Scheme versatility across multiple techniques
least_important_traits: Pure pass-rush specialization without run defense ability
Nose tackle-style two-gapping ability — Ossenfort wants penetrators
deal_breakers: Inconsistent effort or questions about passion for the game (notably, Nolen had 'major concerns around his love/passion for the game' pre-draft but the Cardinals still drafted him, suggesting they resolved the concern through their process)
One-dimensional run stuffers without pass-rush upside
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite college production at DT (sacks and TFL from the interior position) can push a player up Ossenfort's board.

college_production.production_floor: Nolen: 6.5 sacks, 14 TFL in final season. Robinson had 8.5 sacks in his final year playing both DT and edge.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending production. Nolen earned First-Team All-SEC in his final season.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Adjusts for position — sack production from DT alignment is weighted higher than from edge.

examples: 2025 1.16 — Walter Nolen, Ole Miss: 6-3.75, 296. 6.5 sacks, 14 TFL, 32 tackles within 2 yards of LOS. Second-highest PFF run-defense grade in FBS (91.6). First-Team All-SEC. 7th-ranked prospect on PFF's big board.
2024 1.27 — Darius Robinson, Missouri: 6-5, 285. Originally a DT who transitioned to edge. Interior experience informs his versatility. First-Team All-SEC with 8.5 sacks.

CB.archetype_summary: Ossenfort has invested more draft capital at CB than any other position — 7 corners in 3 drafts. The archetype is clear: quick, technically sound corners with good ball skills and zone coverage ability who can play multiple spots in the secondary. Values instincts, ball production, competitive toughness, and willingness to tackle. Has consistently taken corners with injury or concern-related slides, getting higher-talent players at lower cost. Gannon's secondary-coach background heavily influenced CB investment; Rallis's retention suggests continued emphasis.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-10 to 6-1.75. Wide range — from Garrett Williams (5-10, 192) to Will Johnson (6-1.75, 194). No hard height minimum.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard minimum. Williams has 31-inch arms. Melton at 5-11 is on the smaller side. Johnson at 6-1.75 has more traditional size. Range of body types drafted.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed matters — Melton ran sub-4.3 at the combine. Pappoe (LB, but 4.38 speed) and other athletic picks suggest Ossenfort values timed speed. However, Williams did not have elite timed speed and was still a Day 2 pick based on film.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.50 preferred but not a hard cutoff. Values quickness, hip fluidity, and change-of-direction over pure straight-line speed. Williams estimated at 4.50 with ACL rehab. Melton was sub-4.3.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Zone coverage foundational ability. Gannon's scheme used heavy zone concepts, and Rallis continued that emphasis. Cardinals played 440 defensive snaps in zone in 2024 (tied 16th in NFL). Williams was praised for 'off-zone coverage ability.' But also values man-coverage versatility — Johnson was described as a 'lockdown' type who can shadow WR1s.
most_valued_traits: Ball skills and instincts — Melton had 8 career INTs, Williams had multiple per year, Johnson had elite passer rating allowed in coverage
Competitive toughness and willingness to tackle in run support
Fluidity in transitions and hip movement
Ability to play multiple coverage roles (outside, slot, zone, man)
Football IQ and recognition skills
least_important_traits: Elite press-man technique as a prerequisite — zone-first scheme doesn't demand it
Elite size — has drafted CBs from 5-10 to 6-2
deal_breakers: Poor ball skills or inability to create turnovers
Lack of tackling willingness or ability in run support
Character or cultural fit issues — values 'good people'
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite talent that slides due to injury. Williams was coming off a torn ACL and was drafted in round 3 (would have been higher healthy). Will Johnson was a projected top-10 talent who fell to 47 due to knee concerns. Ossenfort 'has a soft spot for talented players that slide.'

college_production.production_floor: No hard statistical floor. Values film over INT numbers. Williams had 4 INTs in college despite ACL-shortened season. Melton had 8 career INTs at Rutgers. Johnson had elite passer rating allowed.
college_production.production_trajectory: Multi-year starters strongly preferred. Williams was a 3-year starter. Melton started multiple years. Johnson was a multi-year contributor at Michigan.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High. Weights quality of competition heavily. All CBs drafted from Power 5 conferences.

examples: 2023 3.72 — Garrett Williams, Syracuse: 5-10, 192, ~4.50 40, 31-inch arms. Three-year starter, torn ACL slide. Excellent ball skills, plus tackler.
2023 5.171 — Kei'Trel Clark, Louisville: Slot-capable nickel DB.
2024 2.43 — Max Melton, Rutgers: 5-11, sub-4.30 40. Eight career INTs, blocked 3 kicks. Speed and ball production.
2024 3.90 — Elijah Jones, Boston College: 6-2, 184. Size and speed enforcer.
2024 7.226 — Jaden Davis, Miami: Versatile DB who can play multiple positions.

2025 2.47 — Will Johnson, Michigan: 6-1.75, 194. Projected top-10 pick who slid due to knee concerns. Lockdown ability, 57.2 passer rating allowed over 3 years (94th percentile). Big, fluid, instinctive.
2025 5.174 — Denzel Burke, Ohio State: 5-11.25, 186. Arizona native. Started every game at OSU. Athleticism and game knowledge.

S.archetype_summary: Ossenfort has not invested premium draft capital at safety, preferring to rely on established veterans (Budda Baker, Jalen Thompson). Late-round picks target athletic, special-teams-capable safeties who can develop. Baker's re-signing through 2027 and Thompson's presence reduce urgency. The profile for drafted safeties emphasizes athleticism, range, and special teams ability as a pathway to the roster.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear threshold from limited data
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Combine performance matters highly for late-round safety picks. Crawford 'showcased athleticism in spades at the NFL Scouting Combine.'
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Above-average speed and range required to play single-high or cover in space.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Versatility and willingness to contribute on special teams first, then earn defensive reps.
most_valued_traits: Elite athleticism and combine performance
Special teams ability and willingness
Positional versatility — ability to play multiple safety spots
Range in coverage
Tackling ability and physical play
least_important_traits: Immediate starting ability — Baker and Thompson handle that
Run-stopping specialization without coverage range
deal_breakers: Poor athleticism or limited range
Unwillingness to contribute on special teams
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: No evidence of reaching for safeties — all picks have been late rounds.

college_production.production_floor: Crawford earned 88.5 PFF coverage grade in his lone starting season at Nevada. Taylor-Demerson was a 4th-round pick from Texas Tech. Production threshold is low given the round of investment.
college_production.production_trajectory: Willing to take late-blooming safeties who transferred for playing time (Crawford transferred from Texas to Nevada for starting opportunity).
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate. Values combine performance heavily for this position group.

examples: 2024 4.104 — Dadrion Taylor-Demerson, Texas Tech: Safety with positional versatility.
2025 7.225 — Kitan Crawford, Nevada: 5-10.75, 202. Athletic prospect who transferred from Texas for playing time. 88.5 PFF coverage grade. Elite combine athlete. Primary special teams pathway.

LB.archetype_summary: Athletic, sideline-to-sideline linebackers who can cover in space and contribute on special teams. Ossenfort values physical traits (elite speed, range) combined with football instincts and production. The profile bridges traditional off-ball linebacker play with the coverage demands of modern NFL defenses. Wants players who can tackle, cover, and rush the passer.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed is king. Owen Pappoe (2023 5th round) ran a 4.38 40 at the combine — an athletic freak. Cody Simon was selected for his production and instincts.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.55 preferred. Pappoe's 4.38 suggests Ossenfort values elite speed at LB.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Sideline-to-sideline range and coverage ability combined with instinctive play and production.
most_valued_traits: Sideline-to-sideline speed and range
Coverage ability — can drop and cover in space
Tackling production and instincts (Simon had 112 tackles and 7 sacks)
Special teams value (Pappoe was targeted as key ST player)
Blitz ability and pass-rush upside
least_important_traits: Pure size and run-stuffing ability — not seeking 250-lb thumpers
Specialization as only a run defender
deal_breakers: Inability to cover in space — modern defense demands it
Lack of speed or range — slow LBs are non-starters
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite combine speed numbers (Pappoe's 4.38 pushed his stock despite being a Day 3 pick). Elite production at a big-time program (Simon was CFP National Championship Defensive MVP).

college_production.production_floor: Simon had 112 tackles and 7 sacks in his final season — rare combination. Pappoe was more of an athletic projection pick.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values proven production. Simon was one of four FBS players with 100+ tackles and 7+ sacks.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Weights program and competition level. Simon from Ohio State in CFP spotlight. Pappoe from Auburn with elite athleticism.

examples: 2023 5.168 — Owen Pappoe, Auburn: 6-1, 225, 4.38 40, 29 bench reps. Elite athletic freak targeted as special teams weapon with LB upside.
2025 4.115 — Cody Simon, Ohio State: 6-2, 229. 112 tackles, 7 sacks. CFP National Championship and Rose Bowl Defensive MVP. Productive, instinctive Mike linebacker.

WR.archetype_summary: When Ossenfort invests in a WR, he goes for elite, franchise-caliber talent. Marvin Harrison Jr. at pick 4 was the consensus #1 non-QB — a generational route runner with contested-catch ability and elite size. At the other end, Michael Wilson (3rd round, 2023) and Tejhaun Palmer (6th round, 2024) represent big-bodied, physical receivers who bring size and toughness. The through-line: size, physicality, and the ability to win at the catch point. Not drafting undersized slot specialists in early rounds.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard minimum but has not drafted a small WR in early rounds
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Route running technique and body control matter more than 40 time. Harrison did not participate in combine workouts. Wilson brings 6-2, 213 frame.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not primary. Harrison's value was route savvy and technique, not timed speed. Palmer had quickness and YAC ability.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Contested-catch ability, route precision, and the ability to play the X (outside) receiver position. Harrison was described as a 'unicorn route savant at over 6-3.'
most_valued_traits: Elite route running and separation ability
Contested-catch dominance at the catch point
Size to play outside and win against press
Physicality and competitiveness
Character and work ethic (Harrison's football pedigree, Wilson's Stanford background)
least_important_traits: Pure deep speed — not drafting track stars without route skills
Slot-only ability — wants outside playmakers
deal_breakers: Poor route running even with elite athleticism
Inability to win at the catch point
Character or professionalism concerns
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Generational talent evaluation. Harrison at 4 was BPA and a need — Ossenfort said the decision to stay was easy given the prospect quality.

college_production.production_floor: Harrison: 2,600+ career yards, 31 TDs at Ohio State. Wilson: limited by injuries at Stanford but showed 6-2, 213 frame and 76 yards/TD at Senior Bowl. Palmer: averaged 2.15 yards per route run at UAB.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values elite production at the highest level. Harrison was a Biletnikoff Award winner. Injury-limited players get a pass if the tape is strong (Wilson).
college_production.context_sensitivity: High. Credited Harrison despite 'step down in quarterback play' at OSU. Valued Wilson's Senior Bowl performance to supplement injury-limited college tape.

examples: 2024 1.4 — Marvin Harrison Jr., Ohio State: 6-4, 209. Consensus #1 non-QB prospect. Biletnikoff Award winner, 2,600+ career yards, 31 TDs. Route savant with contested-catch ability. Generational talent.
2023 3.94 — Michael Wilson, Stanford: 6-2, 213. Big, physical receiver with size and toughness. Injury history but strong intangibles.
2024 6.191 — Tejhaun Palmer, UAB: Productive receiver who averaged 2.15 yards per route run. Low drop rate (2.1%). 8.8 YAC. Day 3 depth pick.

TE.archetype_summary: Blocking-first tight end with athletic upside. Ossenfort's only drafted TE is Tip Reiman, a workout warrior and elite blocker whose receiving resume was minimal in college. With Trey McBride already the Cardinals' All-Pro receiving TE, Ossenfort drafted the perfect complement: a physical in-line blocker who can develop as a receiver. The profile is old-school — physical, tough, and dominant in the run game.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No data from single sample. Reiman had 10.5-inch hands (Gronkowski/Graham range).
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Reiman was described as a 'workout warrior' who 'caught a ton of attention at the Shrine Bowl and NFL scouting combine.' Track athlete background in high school. Athletic testing matters for TE evaluation.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Functional speed to be a viable option in play-action. Not seeking a pure receiving TE given McBride's presence.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Dominant run blocking. Reiman did not allow a single sack in his final college season with just two pressures and three blown blocks in the run game.
most_valued_traits: Elite run-blocking ability and physicality
Competitive toughness — said his favorite part was 'mauling somebody'
Athletic upside from track background
Large hands for reliable receiving potential
Willingness to do the dirty work
least_important_traits: Prolific receiving production in college — Reiman had only 24 targets
Route-running polish
deal_breakers: Inability to block at the NFL level
Softness or lack of physicality at the point of attack
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Athletic combine/Shrine Bowl performance. Reiman was a 'late riser through the pre-draft process' whose stock rose on testing.

college_production.production_floor: Very low for receiving stats. Reiman had 24 targets with zero drops. Production was run-blocking focused. Only 1 TE drafted — low data confidence.
college_production.production_trajectory: N/A — single data point. Reiman was not asked to be a receiving weapon at Illinois.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High. Fully understood that Reiman's limited receiving stats reflected usage, not ability. His blocking film and combine performance drove the evaluation.

examples: 2024 3.82 — Tip Reiman, Illinois: 6-5, 271. Zero sacks allowed, 2 pressures, 3 blown blocks. Workout warrior with track background and 10.5-inch hands. 'Mauler' mentality. Complementary blocking TE to McBride.

TE.confidence_note: Only 1 TE drafted. Profile heavily inferred from scheme needs (McBride as receiving TE means blocking TE is complementary role). Will need more data points to confirm pattern.

RB.archetype_summary: Explosive, power-speed backs who can handle early-down duties and provide a physical complement in the backfield. Trey Benson was the only RB drafted in three years, taken in the 3rd round (pick 66) in 2024. He was one of the top RBs in the class, a physical runner from Florida State with 900+ rushing yards. Ossenfort does not prioritize RB in early rounds but will take one in the mid-rounds when the value is right.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Power metrics and burst. Benson was touted for his physical running style and ability to compete for touches immediately.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Above-average speed to complement power. Benson had the speed-power combination Ossenfort sought.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Physical, downhill running with enough speed to break long runs. Not a receiving-first or scat back.
most_valued_traits: Physical running style and power between the tackles
Speed-power combination
Ability to immediately compete for carries
Durability and toughness
Production behind a quality offensive line
least_important_traits: Receiving ability as primary skill — not drafting pass-catching specialists
Elusiveness over power — wants physical runners
deal_breakers: Lack of physical running ability or willingness to run between the tackles
Pure speed backs without power element
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: No evidence of reaching for RBs. Ossenfort deprioritizes the position in early rounds.

college_production.production_floor: Benson had 900+ rushing yards in his final season at Florida State. Only 1 RB drafted — minimal data.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers productive backs from major programs.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate. Benson was a quality RB prospect at a school known for producing NFL backs.

examples: 2024 3.66 — Trey Benson, Florida State: ~6-0, 215. 900+ rushing yards in final season. Physical runner with speed-power combination. Immediately competed for touches behind James Conner.

RB.confidence_note: Only 1 RB drafted in 3 years. Position is clearly deprioritized in early rounds. Profile represents what Ossenfort will take when value aligns, not a primary target.

QB.archetype_summary: Ossenfort has not invested premium draft capital on a QB in three drafts as Cardinals GM — his only QB selection was Clayton Tune in the 5th round of the 2023 draft. However, the Cardinals are now releasing Kyler Murray and hold the 3rd overall pick in 2026, making QB the dominant storyline. Ossenfort's stated philosophy is 'all options are on the table' at QB. His Patriots background provides the strongest signal: New England valued pocket presence, processing speed, leadership, and poise under Belichick. The hire of Mike LaFleur (Shanahan/McVay tree) suggests the next QB will need to operate in a play-action-heavy, bootleg-and-movement-based offense. The profile is inferred largely from scouting tree and scheme context.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-1 to 6-4 (inferred from Belichick tree preference for adequate size). Clayton Tune was 6-3, 220 from Houston — prototypical size.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength and velocity are likely prioritized based on Belichick tree. But with LaFleur's Shanahan/McVay influence, mobility and the ability to execute bootleg/play-action concepts will be heavily weighted.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not requiring a running QB but wants mobility to move in the pocket, execute bootlegs, and extend plays. Tune was described as 'a good athlete' with 'rushing upside.'

playing_style.primary_style: Play-action and movement-based passing within a Shanahan/McVay-style offense. LaFleur's scheme demands a QB who can sell play-fakes, execute rollouts, and throw with anticipation on intermediate routes. Processing speed and accuracy are paramount.
most_valued_traits: Processing speed and pre-snap reads
Accuracy on intermediate and deep throws
Ability to operate play-action bootleg concepts
Poise under pressure and pocket presence
Leadership and character (Ossenfort's 'ego will not be tolerated' culture)
least_important_traits: Pure rushing ability as a primary weapon — not seeking a run-first QB based on the Murray experience ending
40 time or straight-line speed
deal_breakers: Character or work ethic red flags — Ossenfort's culture demands accountability
Inability to process at NFL speed
Run-first mentality without passing development
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Unknown — no premium QB picks to analyze. With pick 3 in 2026, Ossenfort may trade up or stay for a franchise QB for the first time.

college_production.production_floor: Tune had back-to-back grades above 90.0 at Houston with 79.5% adjusted completion percentage (7th nationally). Multi-year starter.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values consistency and sustained performance. Tune was an experienced passer with significant starting experience.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High. Will adjust for supporting cast and offensive system. LaFleur's evaluation of scheme fit will be the primary filter for 2026 QB prospects.

examples: 2023 5.169 — Clayton Tune, Houston: 6-3, 220. Air-raid system passer with back-to-back 90.0+ PFF grades. Good athlete with rushing upside. 79.5% adjusted completion rate. Developmental pick.

QB.confidence_note: Only 1 QB drafted (Day 3 developmental pick). Profile is heavily inferred from scouting tree (Belichick) and new HC scheme (LaFleur/Shanahan tree). The 2026 draft at pick 3 with Kyler Murray released will be the first true test of Ossenfort's QB evaluation philosophy. This profile should be substantially revised after the 2026 draft.

reach_tolerance.notes: Ossenfort generally drafts close to consensus in round 1. Harrison at 4 was consensus BPA. Johnson at 6 was consensus top-10. Nolen at 16 was 7th on PFF's big board but fell due to character concerns — arguably a value pick. Robinson at 27 was the most notable reach, viewed by some as a late-1st/early-2nd prospect taken at pick 27. In rounds 2-3, Ossenfort shows moderate willingness to reach for scheme fits and sliding talent. His biggest strength is getting value picks — Will Johnson (top-10 talent at pick 47) and Williams (higher talent than pick 72 without injury) represent his preferred approach of capitalizing on others' concerns.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Transitioning to Shanahan/McVay-style offense under Mike LaFleur with Nathaniel Hackett as OC. Will feature wide-zone running concepts, play-action bootlegs, and motion-heavy passing game. Previous offense under Drew Petzing/Jonathan Gannon was described as West Coast. LaFleur's scheme demands athletic OL who can pull and reach-block, mobile TE/FB types, and receivers who can win after the catch. LaFleur will call plays himself.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Multiple-front defense under DC Nick Rallis (retained from Gannon staff). Emphasizes versatility along the defensive front — players who can align at multiple spots. Heavy zone coverage concepts (440 zone snaps in 2024, tied 16th in NFL). Values interior disruption paired with versatile edge rushers. Three seasons of continuity under Rallis.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: MAJOR OFFENSIVE SCHEME CHANGE: Mike LaFleur hired as HC on Feb 1, 2026, replacing Jonathan Gannon. LaFleur comes from the Shanahan/McVay coaching tree — his offense will differ significantly from Gannon/Petzing's system. Defensive scheme has continuity with Rallis retained. Offensive draft targets in 2026 should be evaluated through a Shanahan/McVay lens (zone-run OL, play-action QB, versatile receiving weapons). This is LaFleur's first draft as HC — the GM-HC dynamic on offensive player selection is TBD.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate-to-aggressive risk tolerance, particularly when it comes to injury-flagged players. Has consistently drafted players others avoided due to medical concerns: Garrett Williams (torn ACL), Michael Wilson (injury history), Will Johnson (knee concerns), Walter Nolen (passion/motivation questions). Ossenfort is willing to bet on talent when others are scared off by red flags. However, his non-medical picks tend to be safe, high-floor selections at positions of need.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate. The 2025 draft (6 of 7 picks on defense) was a direct response to the defense ranking 27th in 2024. However, this was also rational roster construction rather than pure reactionary behavior. The 2024 draft featured heavy CB investment after 2023's secondary struggles. Ossenfort appears to draft from a plan but adjusts that plan based on recent performance gaps.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Willing to move on from underperforming investments. Released Kyler Murray despite massive guaranteed money. Made 2024 roster moves indicating willingness to cut losses. Does not double down at positions where picks haven't worked.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Balanced. Ossenfort said 'the vast majority' of evaluation 'is always going to go back to the game tape' but acknowledged the combine fills gaps with medical evaluations, interviews, and physical testing. His draft picks show a mix: some were combine risers (Reiman was a 'workout warrior'), others were drafted purely on film despite limited testing data (Harrison didn't participate in combine drills). An SI analysis noted 'the Cardinals clearly do like their players to be more on the athletic side' based on Relative Athletic Scores of drafted players.
psychological_profile.character_description: Very high. Ossenfort's introductory press conference was dominated by character themes. He said 'ego will not be tolerated' and emphasized finding 'good players and good people.' His Belichick background instilled a strong 'team-first' evaluation framework. He called his Patriots time a 'master class education' and stated 'we are not just collecting talent — we're going to build a team.' Every draft press conference references cultural fit alongside talent evaluation. The Murray release — despite talent — partly reflects Ossenfort's willingness to move on from players who don't fit the culture.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: In the process of defining this for the first time. Has not drafted a premium QB in three years as GM. Inherited Kyler Murray and committed to him initially but ultimately moved on after Murray couldn't stay healthy and the team went 3-14 in 2025. Now holding pick 3 in 2026 with Murray released, Ossenfort faces his defining QB decision. His stated approach: 'all options are on the table.' With LaFleur's hire from the Shanahan/McVay tree, the QB selection will likely prioritize play-action/bootleg fit, processing speed, and pocket mobility over pure arm talent or rushing ability.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Balanced but leans slightly toward production. Harrison was an elite producer. Robinson had ascending production. Simon was ultra-productive (112 tackles, 7 sacks). However, Ossenfort has also made clear projection bets: Reiman was drafted on athletic testing more than receiving production, Pappoe on speed rather than LB stats, and Robinson was still developing as a pass rusher when drafted. When forced to choose, Ossenfort leans toward productive players from elite programs but will project on athleticism when the physical profile is exceptional.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No WR drafted in rounds 1-3 with underwhelming production. Harrison was a Biletnikoff winner. Even Wilson, despite injuries, had strong Senior Bowl showing.
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: Ascending production required. Robinson had 8.5 sacks in final year. Ojulari had 16.5 career sacks. Burch had 11.5 sacks in 2 years.
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No hard statistical floor. Film and passer rating allowed valued over INT totals. But multi-year starting experience is important.
production_thresholds_by_position.DT: Elite production for the position. Nolen had 6.5 sacks from DT — third among all DTs in FBS.
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: High-end production matters. Simon was one of only 4 FBS players with 100+ tackles and 7+ sacks.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: 900+ rushing yards in final season for mid-round picks (Benson).

college_production_preferences.notes: Ossenfort values production as a baseline but is clearly willing to look past limited production when the athletic profile or circumstance (injuries, scheme, position change) explains the gap. His Patriots background showed a willingness to project on traits over production (e.g., Jimmy Garoppolo, Sony Michel). But his own picks as Cardinals GM lean more toward productive college players than pure projection picks.

CB.notes: Has drafted CBs across speed spectrum from Williams (~4.50) to Melton (sub-4.30). Film-first approach at CB. Prefers sub-4.50 but no hard cutoff.

EDGE.notes: Length clearly valued. All early-round EDGE picks have 34+ inch arms.

OT.notes: Johnson had 36 1/8-inch arms — elite length. Only 1 premium OT pick but length was clearly a factor.

DT.notes: Nolen was 6-3.75, 296 — athletic for the position. Athleticism-to-size ratio matters more than any single number.

LB.notes: Pappoe ran 4.38. Speed is clearly valued at LB.

WR.notes: Harrison (6-4), Wilson (6-2). Size matters for outside WR evaluations.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences rather than hard cutoffs. Ossenfort evaluates holistically — his 'pie' metaphor suggests no single measurement is an eliminator. However, arm length for EDGE and OT appears close to a hard threshold based on limited data.

size_preferences_by_position.DT: 295+ for interior DL. Nolen at 296. Robinson at 285 played inside and out.
size_preferences_by_position.CB: No strong size preference — has drafted from 5-10 to 6-2.
size_preferences_by_position.WR: 6-0+ for outside receivers. Undersized slot types not drafted in early rounds.

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Based on the SI analysis noting Cardinals 'clearly do like their players to be more on the athletic side,' athleticism is weighted as a positive signal across all positions. Specific tests: Broad jump and vertical jump for explosiveness (relevant for EDGE, DT, LB). 40-yard dash for speed positions (CB, LB). Combine interviews and medical evaluations are heavily weighted for the Cardinals' character and health evaluation process.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first GM by his own admission. Ossenfort said 'the vast majority is always going to go back to the game tape.' However, combine risers (Reiman) can climb boards, and testing confirms or challenges film evaluations. When film and testing diverge, film wins — Harrison was drafted at 4 without combine workout participation. But athletic testing matters more for Day 2-3 picks where film differentiation is smaller.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium positions, franchise-caliber players. In round 1, Ossenfort has taken: an elite OT (Johnson, 2023), an elite WR (Harrison, 2024), a versatile DL (Robinson, 2024), and a disruptive DT (Nolen, 2025). All were players expected to start immediately and become cornerstones. Ossenfort will trade back from a top pick if the value is there (2023 from 3 to 6) but will stay put for a generational talent (2024 at 4 for Harrison). Not afraid to take a player with some question marks in round 1 if the talent is elite (Nolen's motivation concerns, Robinson's reach label).
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Shifts to need-filling while still targeting value. Heavy CB investment in rounds 2-3 across all three drafts. This is where Ossenfort capitalizes on sliding talent: Williams (torn ACL slide to 72), Will Johnson (knee concerns slide to 47). Also uses Day 2 for positional building blocks: Benson at RB, Adams at IOL, Reiman at TE, Burch at EDGE. Willing to take back-to-back picks at the same position (3 CBs in 2024, 2 CBs in 2025). Ossenfort turned down trade-back offers at picks 47 and 78 in 2025 when he had specific targets — won't sacrifice a player he wants for marginal pick accumulation.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, special teams contributors, and depth pieces. Pappoe (elite speed LB for special teams), Gaines (reliable IOL depth), Tune (developmental QB), Crawford (athletic safety for special teams), Burke (CB depth with local ties), Palmer (productive Day 3 WR). Ossenfort uses Day 3 to add depth and find players who can earn roster spots through special teams contribution. Character and program pedigree (Ohio State preference evident) matter here.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: No clear pattern of treating comp picks differently. Has used acquired picks from trades similarly to original picks. All picks appear to be treated equally on the board.

OT.note: Paris Johnson Jr. became immediate starter at RT. Adams and Jones are depth. Still early in tenure to fully evaluate.

EDGE.note: Ojulari started 17 games as a rookie with 4 sacks before torn ACL in 2024. Robinson was injured in 2024 but played 15 games in 2025. Burch and Thomas are still developing. Early returns mixed due to injuries.

CB.note: Williams became a quality starter. Will Johnson projected as CB1. Melton, Jones, Burke, Clark, and Davis are depth/developmental. Heavy investment but too early to evaluate most picks. Volume approach suggests Ossenfort expects some attrition.

WR.note: Harrison Jr. is the cornerstone WR. Wilson has been a contributor but not a star. Palmer is a depth piece. Harrison pick is a clear win.

DT.note: Nolen is a 2025 rookie — too early to evaluate. High expectations given the 16th overall pick.

RB.note: Benson competed for touches behind Conner. Contributor but not yet a starter.

LB.note: Pappoe primarily a special teamer. Simon is a 2025 rookie.

TE.note: Reiman is a blocking TE behind All-Pro McBride. Fulfilling his role.

IOL.note: Gaines has started games. Adams and Conner are developmental.

blind_spots: Too early in tenure (only 3 drafts) to identify definitive blind spots. Injury-prone picks (Williams torn ACL, Ojulari torn ACL, Robinson calf injury in 2024) are a pattern worth monitoring — Ossenfort's willingness to draft injured players means a higher variance outcome set.
strengths: Capitalizing on sliding talent — consistently gets higher-talent players at lower cost by being willing to accept medical/character risk that other teams avoid. Round 1 picks have been widely praised as strong selections. Trade execution in 2023 was masterful.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Moderate and targeted spender in free agency. Uses FA to fill specific veteran needs (Josh Sweat at EDGE, Calais Campbell on DL, Bilal Nichols, Sean Murphy-Bunting at CB, Jacoby Brissett at QB) while building the core through the draft. Not a big-splash FA team. Ossenfort said 'the draft has gotta be where we build and where this team forms the foundation.'
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When veterans are signed at a position, Ossenfort does NOT stop drafting that position. Signed Murphy-Bunting at CB before 2024 draft, then drafted 3 CBs. Signed Josh Sweat at EDGE in 2025 FA, then still drafted Jordan Burch at EDGE. Signed DL veterans (Campbell, Jones, Tomlinson, Nichols), then drafted Nolen in round 1. Ossenfort believes in stacking positions with both FA and draft capital to create competition.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at every position. Supplement with targeted FA signings of veteran starters and depth. The Cardinals' identity 'resembles a draft and develop team' per the team's own site. Ossenfort's rebuild approach: stockpile picks via trades in year 1, use that capital to add foundational players in years 2-3, supplement with strategic FA. Now entering year 4 with a new HC — the strategy shifts toward winning.

notable_patterns: 7 of 28 picks (25%) have been cornerbacks — by far the most-invested position
4 first-round picks in 3 drafts have been OT (Johnson), WR (Harrison), DL (Robinson), and DT (Nolen) — premium positions only
Has taken an Ohio State player in every draft year: Johnson (2023), Harrison (2024), Simon and Burke (2025)
Consistently targets talented players who slide due to injury or character concerns at significant value
Heavy defensive investment: 2025 draft was 6 of 7 picks on defense
Has never drafted a QB in rounds 1-4 as Cardinals GM (Tune was 5th round)
Executed masterful 2023 trade sequence: 3→12 (gaining future 1st) then 12→6, getting Paris Johnson Jr. AND Houston's 2024 1st
Turned down trade-back offers in 2025 at picks 47 and 78 to take specific targets

biggest_reaches.context: Robinson's DT-to-edge versatility pushed him up the Cardinals' board. Some analysts viewed him as a late-1st/early-2nd prospect. Ossenfort valued the scheme versatility and power profile.

biggest_steals.context: Johnson was a projected top-10 pick whose knee concerns caused a massive slide. Ossenfort, true to his pattern of drafting sliding talent, grabbed a potential CB1 at pick 47. ESPN called him 'the steal of the draft.'
Williams was projected higher before his torn ACL. Ossenfort's willingness to draft injured players got him a quality starter at a discount.

confidence_notes: 3 drafts of data as Cardinals GM provide a meaningful sample but still limited. Ossenfort's tendencies are becoming clear: values versatility, targets sliding talent, invests heavily in defensive secondary and front, leans BPA with scheme-fit overlay, embraces trade flexibility. However, the 2026 draft will be his most consequential — first major QB decision, first draft with a new HC (LaFleur), and first draft holding a top-3 pick since the 2023 trade-down. The LaFleur hire adds significant uncertainty to offensive player evaluation. Defensive draft tendencies have high confidence; offensive tendencies and QB philosophy have low confidence and will need substantial revision after 2026.
